In case you are wondering, Fervo Energy co-founder and CTO Dr. @JackNorbeck received his PhD in Stanford’s Geothermal Reservoir Engineering group and was already a world recognized expert in geothermal with dozens of publications before he co-founded Fervo.

In any hardtech startup there is a singular discipline that ties the tech to the economics and it’s vital a founding team has that as a core competency.

In geothermal, it’s Reservoir Engineering, and there are countless startups working on bad ideas because they lack that skill.

Also people don’t realize how extraordinarily time consuming it is. At a certain point exec teams spend much of their time fundraising. 

An under appreciated success factor of the Musk companies is exec teams get to be way more focused since capital is easier to come by.
And the numbers of rejections just go up the more success people find! Because more funding rounds always mean way more rejections than people who haven’t been in the Founder seat realize.

It takes a pretty unique personality to make it as a hardtech founder. Most everyone I know who has found success has been rejected by many hundreds of VCs while fundraising over the years and still finds grit to move forward.
I think it’s practically self evident that this is the value maximizing outcome for society and we should really be thinking about grid planning through a totally different lens.

In case anyone wants to debate me on this topic I present my opening argument below.

The private sector is remarkable at efficiently allocating capital for near term, relatively low risk investments, but has proven ill equipped at making transformative multi-decade bets on its own.

Behind every major breakthrough is gov funding and often huge customer orders.

Arguing whether the private or public sector is more efficient at using capital is a false dichotomy.

The correct answer is that breakthroughs come from blending the best of private market efficiency with public sector support and the debate should be about the right blend.

Anyone in LA area interested in this amada sheet metal laser?

C02 lasers are outdated due to cheap import fibers but this is a decent entry level machine considering we are practically giving it away (low 5 figures, pay your own shipping, we will handle rigging onto a truck)

It also comes with its own nitrogen generation system (and air compressor) so you don’t need to pay for cutting gas.

This machine served us well, still running parts cut quality is amazing. Would keep it around if we could but we need the floor space for our new 10kw machine.

when you put mit researchers, boston dynamics, uc berkeley, princeton, and ex-spacex founders around one robotics dinner table…

some of the best takes from dinner last night:
- tactile sensing is non-negotiable for dexterous manipulation, especially in unstructured environments where the robot can’t rely on vision alone
- world models > pure imitation learning for generalization
- simple grippers + great software can go surprisingly far
- the real bottleneck isn’t architecture, it’s data infrastructure

the debate that kept coming up: are we chasing the cutting edge too fast when there’s so much value left to extract from what already exists?

RT @twistartups: Raspberry Pi gets a comeback as the Star Trek communicator with OpenClaw!
Raspberry Pi was popular in the 2010s and now fa…
RT @twistartups: He made an OpenClaw device smaller than your iPhone! @basti_vkl was on the show today to show off his viral personal OpenC…

built my own personal assistent device that runs OpenClaw.

I was curious what the smallest form factor could be that fits in my pocket so I wanted to use the Pi Zero W.

Works via Push to Talk->Transcribe->Sends to OpenClaw and streams the response back. https://t.co/JCYaltZ5mf
https://t.co/OkMdoyiWD5
update on the 3d printed case https://t.co/FZqJUjxl82
Doing some more variations now https://t.co/oMDd8SBAPf
files available here: https://t.co/qdB6A5gysf
you can just do things on the weekend https://t.co/NxUShWkIc9
it can also talk now and has a tamagotchi like character. updated the repo https://t.co/kxirof1TZM

Hardware
•Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W
•WhisPlay board (screen + button + LED)
•WM8960 mic/speaker
•PiSugar battery

Stack it. Flash Raspberry Pi OS. Enable SSH. Install audio drivers. Confirm mic and speaker work.

Networking
•Install Tailscale on the Pi.
•Rent a small DigitalOcean (or Hetzner or whatever) droplet.
•Install and run OpenClaw on the droplet.
•Bind OpenClaw to localhost.
•Expose it to your tailnet via Tailscale Serve.
•Protect it with a token.

Now the Pi can securely reach your cloud LLM.

Software on the Pi
•Python app.
•Record audio when button pressed.
•Stop recording when released.
•Send audio to OpenAI for transcription.
•Send transcript to OpenClaw.
•Stream response back.
•Display text on LCD.
•Optionally send text to OpenAI TTS and play audio.
•Maintain simple conversation history.
•Use a state machine for: idle, listening, thinking, streaming.

Deployment
•Develop locally.
•Sync to Pi with rsync.
•Run as systemd service so it starts on boot.
•Auto-restart on crash.

Power
•Install PiSugar manager.
•Enable auto power on.
•Use display sleep for inactivity.

That’s the system:
Button → record → transcribe → cloud LLM → stream back → display/speak → idle.

I made LLMs play Monopoly against each other to evaluate their strategic thinking and dealmaking ability.  Here are the results:

(thread) https://t.co/qxQLPZIRdF
Overall, while Opus was the clear winner, all the LLMs were playing very sub-optimally, generally by not being aggressive enough to monopolize and jack up rents.

This eval had several limitations due to high token cost (Monopoly games run 100+ turns with massive board context):

1) All models played at their lowest reasoning setting.  A higher thinking budget could change things a lot
2) All games were head-to-head—multiplayer games would introduce more interesting dealmaking dynamics
3) Too few models were tested

I see MonopolyBench as a proof of concept and not the final word.  I hope to expand to more models and 3-/4-player games once I have API credits for other models and higher rate limits!

Claude Research is so much better than ChatGPT Deep Research

- Much deeper research (728 sources vs 16 for the same question)
- No slop follow-up questions
- More concise reports that aren't just information dumps (and which are shorter when there's just not much info)
- Actually works (half my deep research queries simply fail)

the netherlands just made holding equities basically illegal, pretty wow

first of all, eroding 36% of stock appreciation every year will drive tons of money out of the stock market (that's a *huge* compounding friction!), pushing down asset prices, even if things are otherwise going fine

but then you realize you have to pay cap gains on the sale to raise the cash to pay the tax

and the *real* risk is that you get assessed end of the year and pay taxes months later, and if it goes down (which markets do from time to time!) you may owe more taxes than you even had in total value!

the dutch invented the stock market! how times change
